patient_history.chief_complaint: rapidly enlarging pulsatile mass on medial right upper extremity in a 41-day-old infant

input.patient_transcript:
Doctor: Hi, how are you today?
Parent: I'm good, thanks.
Doctor: Great. Can you tell me what brought you here today?
Parent: My baby is here for evaluation of a rapidly enlarging mass on his right arm.
Doctor: I see. Can you tell me a bit more about your baby?
Parent: He's a 41-day-old male infant with hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy.
Doctor: And when was the mass first noticed?
Parent: It was about 5 hours ago.
Doctor: And has it been rapidly enlarging since then?
Parent: Yes, it has been getting bigger.
Doctor: Has your baby had any previous medical procedures on that arm?
Parent: Yes, he had a PICC line placed there during the neonatal period for treatment of Lennox-Gastaut syndrome without status epilepticus.
Doctor: Understood. What did you notice on the physical examination?
Parent: The mass was pulsatile, but his capillary refill was within 2 seconds, pulses were good in all extremities, his arms and legs were warm, and there was no discoloration or swelling.
Doctor: We obtained a CT angiogram, which showed a pseudoaneurysm of the right distal brachial artery measuring about 1.8 by 1.6 by 2.1 cm.
Parent: Okay.
Doctor: An MRI then showed a 2.1 by 1.7 by 1.1 cm mass along the right brachial artery with vascular flow, consistent with a pseudoaneurysm.
Parent: I see.
Doctor: Because of concern for a vascular abnormality related to the prior line, we admitted him to the neonatal intensive care unit for observation. Over the next two days the mass continued to grow, and plastic surgery and vascular surgery were consulted. Your baby is scheduled for microscope-assisted brachial artery repair with both teams involved.
Parent: Thank you for explaining.
input.chart_notes: 39-week term, 41-day-old male infant with hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy presented to the ED with a rapidly enlarging, pulsatile mass on the medial aspect of the right upper extremity. Parents first noticed the mass 5 hours before arrival; it had enlarged rapidly. Neurovascular exam showed a warm, well-perfused limb with 2+ brachial, radial, and ulnar pulses, capillary refill <2 seconds, no discoloration or edema, and age-appropriate movement of all extremities. Past history notable for PICC placement in the same region during the neonatal period for treatment of Lennox-Gastaut syndrome without status epilepticus. CT angiography demonstrated a pseudoaneurysm of the right distal brachial artery measuring approximately 1.8 × 1.6 × 2.1 cm with clear communication to the brachial artery lumen and no other vascular abnormalities. Contrast-enhanced MRI confirmed a 2.1 × 1.7 × 1.1 cm mass along the right brachial artery with vascular flow. The infant was admitted to the NICU for observation; over 2 days the mass continued to grow, and plastic and vascular surgery teams were consulted. Under general anesthesia, the pseudoaneurysm was exposed via curvilinear incision at the elbow, the diseased segment resected, and a tension-free end-to-end microvascular anastomosis of the brachial artery (2 mm diameter) was performed with 9-0 nylon. Postoperatively, the infant received low-dose intravenous heparin (10 U/kg/h) for 48 hours, had warm, well-perfused upper extremities without bleeding, and was discharged on postoperative day 3 with an arm splint and oral acetaminophen suspension for pain. At 2-year follow-up, the surgical site was well healed, brachial/ulnar/radial pulses were symmetric, and right upper extremity function was normal.

prediction.clinical_summary: 41-day-old term male infant with hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy and Lennox-Gastaut syndrome presented with a 5-hour history of a rapidly enlarging pulsatile mass on the medial right upper extremity, at the site of prior neonatal PICC placement. Exam showed a warm, well-perfused limb with capillary refill <2 seconds, 2+ brachial/radial/ulnar pulses, no discoloration or edema, and intact age-appropriate movement; CTA and MRI confirmed a right distal brachial artery pseudoaneurysm measuring approximately 2 cm. He was admitted to the NICU for observation, and with continued enlargement underwent microscope-assisted resection of the diseased brachial artery segment with tension-free end-to-end microvascular repair; postoperative medications included low-dose IV heparin for 48 hours, and he was discharged on postoperative day 3 with an arm splint and oral acetaminophen.

differential_diagnosis.condition: Iatrogenic right brachial artery pseudoaneurysm secondary to prior PICC placement
differential_diagnosis.rationale: The rapidly enlarging pulsatile upper-extremity mass at the prior PICC site with CTA/MRI confirmation of a 2 cm distal brachial artery pseudoaneurysm makes this the most likely diagnosis.

differential_diagnosis.condition: Traumatic or catheter-associated brachial artery aneurysmal injury with contained hematoma
differential_diagnosis.rationale: Prior arterial wall injury from neonatal line placement can produce a pulsatile mass from a contained arterial leak or evolving aneurysmal change even when distal perfusion remains intact.

differential_diagnosis.condition: Arteriovenous fistula of the right upper extremity related to vascular access
differential_diagnosis.rationale: A vascular-access complication such as an AV fistula can present as a warm, enlarging pulsatile extremity mass with preserved pulses and should be considered before imaging definitively identifies a pseudoaneurysm.

final_report.subjective: 41-day-old term male infant was brought for evaluation of a rapidly enlarging mass on the medial right upper extremity. Parents reported first noticing the mass about 5 hours prior to presentation, with progressive enlargement since onset. The infant has a past medical history significant for hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy and Lennox-Gastaut syndrome without status epilepticus. Parents noted that a PICC line had previously been placed in the same right arm during the neonatal period. No drug allergies were reported.
final_report.objective: On examination, the right upper extremity mass was pulsatile. The limb remained warm and well perfused with capillary refill less than 2 seconds, 2+ brachial, radial, and ulnar pulses, no discoloration, no edema, and age-appropriate movement of the extremities. CT angiography demonstrated a pseudoaneurysm of the right distal brachial artery measuring approximately 1.8 x 1.6 x 2.1 cm with communication to the brachial artery lumen and no other vascular abnormalities. Contrast-enhanced MRI confirmed a 2.1 x 1.7 x 1.1 cm vascular mass along the right brachial artery consistent with pseudoaneurysm. The infant was admitted to the NICU for observation; over the next 2 days the mass continued to enlarge. Plastic surgery and vascular surgery were consulted. He subsequently underwent microscope-assisted resection of the diseased brachial artery segment with tension-free end-to-end microvascular repair of the approximately 2 mm brachial artery under general anesthesia. Postoperatively he received low-dose IV heparin at 10 U/kg/h for 48 hours, maintained warm well-perfused upper extremities without bleeding, and was discharged on postoperative day 3 with an arm splint and oral acetaminophen. At 2-year follow-up, the incision was well healed, pulses were symmetric, and right upper extremity function was normal.
final_report.assessment: This is a 41-day-old male infant with hypoxic-ischemic encephalopathy and Lennox-Gastaut syndrome who developed a rapidly enlarging pulsatile mass at the site of prior neonatal PICC placement in the right upper extremity. Imaging confirmed a right distal brachial artery pseudoaneurysm of approximately 2 cm, most consistent with an iatrogenic brachial artery pseudoaneurysm secondary to prior vascular access. Distal perfusion and neurologic function remained intact on presentation, but interval enlargement over 2 days increased concern for progression and risk of rupture, thrombosis, embolization, or limb compromise. Differential considerations included catheter-associated arterial injury with contained hematoma or aneurysmal change and arteriovenous fistula, but CTA and MRI findings supported pseudoaneurysm as the working and definitive diagnosis.
final_report.plan: Admit to NICU for close neurovascular observation and serial monitoring of size and perfusion of the right upper extremity. Obtain specialty input from plastic surgery and vascular surgery due to enlarging brachial artery pseudoaneurysm in a neonate. Proceed with operative management given continued enlargement: microscope-assisted exploration, resection of the diseased arterial segment, and tension-free end-to-end microvascular brachial artery repair. Postoperatively, continue low-dose IV heparin infusion for 48 hours, monitor for bleeding, recurrent ischemia, thrombosis, or recurrence of the mass, and maintain limb protection with an arm splint. Provide oral acetaminophen suspension as needed for pain control. Discharge when perfusion remains stable and recovery is uncomplicated, with instructions for splint care, return precautions for swelling, discoloration, cool extremity, bleeding, or recurrent mass, and arrange surgical follow-up. Long-term follow-up should include assessment of pulse symmetry, scar healing, and right upper extremity growth and function.
